articles.title: Latitude Ditches the Name Zephyr for Its Two-Stage Rocket
articles.summary:
French launch startup Latitude has removed all mentions of the Zephyr name from its website, now referring to its rocket simply as “Our Launcher”. The rocket, previously known as Zephyr, is a two-stage launch vehicle that will stand 19 metres tall and is designed to deliver up to 200 kilograms to low Earth orbit. The […]
The post Latitude Ditches the Name Zephyr for Its Two-Stage Rocket appeared first on European Spaceflight.

articles.title: Black Eye Galaxy
articles.summary: This March 20, 2026, image of Messier 64, or the Black Eye Galaxy, is a composite view from NASA’s Hubble Space Telescope and James Webb Space Telescope. It shows Messier 64 captured at near- and mid-infrared wavelengths by Webb, while Hubble’s image shows the galaxy in ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared light. Messier 64 is characterized […]

articles.title: H3 successfully returns to flight
articles.summary:
Japan’s H3 rocket launched June 11 on its first flight since a failure in December, placing six smallsats in orbit.
The post H3 successfully returns to flight appeared first on SpaceNews.

articles.title: Hubble Sees Swarm of Galaxies
articles.summary: Looking somewhat like a swarm of bees returning to their hive, this NASA Hubble Space Telescope image features the galaxy cluster MACS0329-0211.

articles.title: Mission Control | Keeping Columbus Running 24/7 | ESA Explores #19
articles.summary: Step inside the Columbus Control Centre near Munich, Germany, and discover what it takes to keep ESA's Columbus laboratory running—24 hours a day, 365 days a year.

articles.title: Earth from Space: Buenos Aires
articles.summary: This radar image from the Copernicus Sentinel-1 mission captures Buenos Aires, the capital of Argentina, the surrounding countryside and the Rio de la Plata estuary.
